lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.
keyIdeas: To identify and correctly apply internationally recognised SI base and derived units for both general physical and specific electrical quantities as required by C&G 2365.
The definition of the Système International (SI) and the distinction between fundamental base units (like the Metre or Second) and derived units (like Velocity or Area).

These are the 'big four' quantities used daily for circuit design, fault finding, and component selection.

steps.title: The SI System Framework
steps.objective: Distinguish between base and derived SI units.
keyIdeas: The definition of the Système International (SI) and the distinction between fundamental base units (like the Metre or Second) and derived units (like Velocity or Area).
Standardisation ensures that an electrical measurement in London means the same thing in Paris or New York, preventing catastrophic engineering errors.

The Système International (SI) is the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering. It ensures that a measurement taken in one country is identical in value to one taken elsewhere, preventing errors in design and safety. Within this system, we distinguish between base units and derived units.

Base units are the fundamental building blocks that cannot be broken down further. Examples include the Metre (m) for length or the Ampere (A) for current. Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units.
Explanation A

Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units.

steps.title: General Physical Units
steps.objective: Identify SI units for length, mass, time, temperature, and velocity.
keyIdeas: Standard units for length (m), area (m²), volume (m³), mass (kg), density (kg/m³), time (s), temperature (K/°C), and velocity (m/s).
Electricians don't just work with wires; we calculate volumes for containment, weights for fixings, and distances for voltage drop.

Electricians must use standard SI units for physical measurements to ensure calculations for containment and cable runs are accurate. Length is measured in Metres (m). When we calculate the space inside a trunking or the surface of a wall, we use Area, measured in square metres (m²). For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).

Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s).
Explanation B
anchorFacts: Electricians must use standard SI units for physical measurements to ensure calculations for containment and cable runs are accurate. Length is measured in Metres (m). When we calculate the space inside a trunking or the surface of a wall, we use Area, measured in square metres (m²). For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).

Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s).
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: Imperial units like inches or pounds are acceptable in technical SI calculations. | Correct belief: Only SI units should be used for formal electrical science calculations.
Remember that area and volume are derived from the metre, while mass is always in kilograms for SI calculations.

steps.title: Core Electrical Quantities
steps.objective: Identify units for current, voltage, resistance, and power.
keyIdeas: The Ampere (A), Volt (V), Ohm (Ω), and Watt (W).
These are the 'big four' quantities used daily for circuit design, fault finding, and component selection.

There are four primary electrical quantities used in every circuit calculation. Current is the flow of electrons, measured in Amperes (A), often shortened to 'Amps'. Voltage is the electrical pressure or potential difference that pushes the current, measured in Volts (V). Resistance is the opposition to that flow of current, measured in Ohms (Ω).

Finally, Power is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into another form, such as heat or light, and is measured in Watts (W). It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. These four units form the foundation of Ohm’s Law and Power Law calculations used to size cables and protection devices.
Teach the core idea: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units.

It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. steps.title: Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity
steps.objective: Determine the units for frequency, energy, and resistivity.
keyIdeas: The Hertz (Hz) for frequency, Joule (J) for energy, and Ohm-metre (Ωm) for resistivity.
Resistivity is a material property essential for calculating cable resistance, while frequency is critical for AC system stability.

Beyond the basic four quantities, we must understand how electricity behaves over time and within different materials. Frequency describes how many times an alternating current (AC) completes a full cycle every second; this is measured in Hertz (Hz). Energy represents the total amount of work done over a period of time and is measured in Joules (J).

While resistance (Ohms) tells us the opposition of a specific component, Resistivity is a property of the material itself, such as copper or aluminium. Resistivity is measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm). This value allows us to calculate the resistance of any length or thickness of wire made from that specific material. Using the correct unit ensures we do not confuse the physical property of the metal with the final resistance of the finished cable.
Teach the core idea: Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities.

steps.title: AC Specific Quantities
steps.objective: Identify units for impedance, reactance, and power factor.
keyIdeas: Impedance (Z) and Reactance (X) measured in Ohms (Ω); Inductance (H); Capacitance (F); and Power Factor (dimensionless/ratio).
In AC circuits, resistance isn't the only thing opposing current; we must account for the effects of coils and capacitors.

In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, current is opposed by more than just standard resistance. We also encounter Reactance (X), which is caused by components like coils and capacitors; this is also measured in Ohms (Ω). The total combined opposition from both resistance and reactance is called Impedance (Z), which is also measured in Ohms (Ω).

The components that cause reactance have their own specific units: Inductance is measured in Henrys (H) and Capacitance is measured in Farads (F). Finally, we use the Power Factor to describe how effectively the circuit is performing. Unlike other quantities, Power Factor is a ratio between 0 and 1. Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all.
Teach the core idea: 202.LO2.AC2.1.

Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Correctly identify and apply SI units to a set of circuit measurements.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a motor circuit and have recorded four different values. To complete the commissioning certificate, you must assign the correct SI unit or symbol to each value: the flow of electrons (15), the electrical pressure (230), the supply cycles per second (50), and the power efficiency ratio (0.8).

Step 1: Identify the electrical quantity for each value. The flow of electrons is Current. The electrical pressure is Voltage. The cycles per second is Frequency. The power efficiency ratio is the Power Factor.
Step 2: Match each quantity to its internationally recognised SI unit or symbol.
1. Current is measured in Amperes (A).
2. Voltage is measured in Volts (V).

steps.title: Motor Specification Analysis
steps.objective: Synthesize the understanding of material properties versus circuit performance by correctly identifying and applying SI units for resistivity and impedance.
keyIdeas: Integrative

When specifying components like motors, we must distinguish between the physical properties of the materials used and the overall electrical behavior of the completed circuit. For example, the copper used in the windings has a specific resistivity, while the entire motor winding provides a total opposition to alternating current known as impedance.
Confusing these two quantities—or their units—can lead to incorrect cable sizing or motor selection.
Using one short explanation, explain why Ωm is the correct technical answer for this 0.0172 is Resistivity, measured in Ohm-metres. scenario.

For example, the copper used in the windings has a specific resistivity, while the entire motor winding provides a total opposition to alternating current known as impedance.
Confusing these two quantities—or their units—can lead to incorrect cable sizing or motor selection.
steps.questionIntent: Synthesize the understanding of material properties versus circuit performance by correctly identifying and applying SI units for resistivity and impedance.

taskSkeleton.scenario: When specifying components like motors, we must distinguish between the physical properties of the materials used and the overall electrical behavior of the completed circuit. For example, the copper used in the windings has a specific resistivity, while the entire motor winding provides a total opposition to alternating current known as impedance.
steps: Confusing these two quantities—or their units—can lead to incorrect cable sizing or motor selection.

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why Ωm is the correct technical answer for this 0.0172 is Resistivity, measured in Ohm-metres. scenario.
steps.hint: Remember that resistivity describes the metal itself (Ohm-metres), while impedance (Z) describes the total 'fight' against AC current in the finished motor (Ohms).

Grounded source text:
Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO2 - Standard units of measurement

Lesson: 202-2A - SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Topic focus: SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Prerequisites: 202-1A

Must-have topics: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO2.AC2.1: Identify and use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units
- 202.LO2.AC2.2: Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Lesson split rationale: Groups the identification of base SI units with specific electrical quantities to establish a standard measurement framework.

In-scope teaching anchors: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Out-of-scope topics: Measuring Electrical Quantities

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (d463ec05-3286-49ee-a12b-b7336612ef31): ## LO2 — Standard units of measurement **AC** 1. Identify and use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units 2. Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities 3. Identify appropriate instruments for measuring different electrical quantities **Range** - SI units for: length; area; volume; mass; density; time; temperature; velocity - Electrical quantities (SI): resistance; resistivity; power; frequency; current; voltage; energy; impedance; inductance/inductive reactance; capacitance/capacitive reactance; power factor - Electrical quantities (measurement): resistance; power; current; voltage; energy ---

      "misconceptions": [

      "objective": "Identify SI units for length, mass, time, temperature, and velocity.",

      "whyItMatters": "Electricians don't just work with wires; we calculate volumes for containment, weights for fixings, and distances for voltage drop.",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Wrong belief: Imperial units like inches or pounds are acceptable in technical SI calculations. | Correct belief: Only SI units should be used for formal electrical science calculations."
      ]
    },
    {
      "key": "core-electrical-units",
      "title": "Core Electrical Quantities",
      "objective": "Identify units for current, voltage, resistance, and power.",

      "misconceptions": [
        "Wrong belief: Power and Energy are the same thing. | Correct belief: Power is the rate of doing work (Watts), while Energy is the total work done over time (Joules)."

      "title": "Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity",

      "misconceptions": [
        "Wrong belief: Resistivity is measured in Ohms. | Correct belief: Resistance is Ohms; Resistivity is Ohm-metres (Ωm), representing the property of the material itself."

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: The international system of measurement used to ensure consistency across engineering and science.
terms.misconception: Thinking imperial units like inches or pounds are used in technical electrical calculations.

terms.simpleDefinition: Fundamental units that are not built from other units, such as the Metre (length) and Ampere (current).
terms.anchor: The fundamental building blocks of measurement.
terms.misconception: Believing that all common units, like the Watt, are base units.

terms.simpleDefinition: Units created by mathematically combining base units, such as the Watt, Volt, or Joule.
terms.anchor: Units built from base unit combinations.
terms.misconception: Thinking derived units are less 'official' or accurate than base units.

terms.simpleDefinition: A property of a specific material that describes how strongly it opposes current flow, regardless of its shape.
terms.misconception: Confusing resistivity (a material property) with resistance (the opposition of a specific object).

terms.simpleDefinition: A ratio showing how effectively electrical power is being converted into useful work in an AC circuit.
terms.misconception: Trying to assign a unit like 'Watts' to power factor when it is actually a unitless ratio.

terms.simpleDefinition: The total opposition to current flow in an AC circuit, including both resistance and reactance.
terms.misconception: Thinking resistance is the only thing that opposes current in an AC system.

terms.simpleDefinition: The total amount of work done or heat produced over a specific period of time.
terms.misconception: Using the terms 'Power' and 'Energy' as if they mean the same thing.

terms.simpleDefinition: The number of complete cycles an alternating current (AC) makes in one second.
terms.misconception: Confusing the frequency of the supply with the speed at which electrons travel.

anchorPhrases: SI units provide a universal standard for the trade.
Base units are the foundation; derived units are the combinations.
Resistance is measured in Ohms, but resistivity is Ohm-metres.
Power factor is a ratio and has no unit.
Impedance (Z) is the total opposition in AC circuits.
misconceptionTargets: Using imperial measurements in electrical science formulas.
Assuming all electrical units are base units.

issues.problem: The planning phase could not extract enough concrete in-scope anchors from the source.
issues.suggestion: Strengthen the source text so the planner can identify multiple concrete teaching anchors.

teachChecks.title: The SI System Framework
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between base and derived SI units.
retrievalTextLines: The Système International (SI) is the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering. It ensures that a measurement taken in one country is identical in value to one taken elsewhere, preventing errors in design and safety. Within this system, we distinguish between base units and derived units.

Base units are the fundamental building blocks that cannot be broken down further. Examples include the Metre (m) for length or the Ampere (A) for current. Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units.
teachChecks.hint: Think about which units are 'standalone' building blocks and which ones are built by combining others.

teachChecks.title: General Physical Units
teachChecks.objective: Identify SI units for length, mass, time, temperature, and velocity.
retrievalTextLines: Electricians must use standard SI units for physical measurements to ensure calculations for containment and cable runs are accurate. Length is measured in Metres (m). When we calculate the space inside a trunking or the surface of a wall, we use Area, measured in square metres (m²). For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).

Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s).
teachChecks.hint: Remember that area and volume are derived from the metre, while mass is always in kilograms for SI calculations.

teachChecks.title: Core Electrical Quantities
teachChecks.objective: Identify units for current, voltage, resistance, and power.
retrievalTextLines: There are four primary electrical quantities used in every circuit calculation. Current is the flow of electrons, measured in Amperes (A), often shortened to 'Amps'. Voltage is the electrical pressure or potential difference that pushes the current, measured in Volts (V). Resistance is the opposition to that flow of current, measured in Ohms (Ω).

Finally, Power is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into another form, such as heat or light, and is measured in Watts (W). It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. These four units form the foundation of Ohm’s Law and Power Law calculations used to size cables and protection devices.
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the 'big four': Amps for flow, Volts for pressure, Ohms for opposition, and Watts for the rate of work.

teachChecks.title: Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity
teachChecks.objective: Determine the units for frequency, energy, and resistivity.
retrievalTextLines: Beyond the basic four quantities, we must understand how electricity behaves over time and within different materials. Frequency describes how many times an alternating current (AC) completes a full cycle every second; this is measured in Hertz (Hz). Energy represents the total amount of work done over a period of time and is measured in Joules (J).

While resistance (Ohms) tells us the opposition of a specific component, Resistivity is a property of the material itself, such as copper or aluminium. Resistivity is measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm). This value allows us to calculate the resistance of any length or thickness of wire made from that specific material. Using the correct unit ensures we do not confuse the physical property of the metal with the final resistance of the finished cable.
teachChecks.hint: Frequency is about cycles per second, Energy is total work, and Resistivity is a material property measured in Ohm-metres.

teachChecks.title: AC Specific Quantities
teachChecks.objective: Identify units for impedance, reactance, and power factor.
retrievalTextLines: In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, current is opposed by more than just standard resistance. We also encounter Reactance (X), which is caused by components like coils and capacitors; this is also measured in Ohms (Ω). The total combined opposition from both resistance and reactance is called Impedance (Z), which is also measured in Ohms (Ω).

The components that cause reactance have their own specific units: Inductance is measured in Henrys (H) and Capacitance is measured in Farads (F). Finally, we use the Power Factor to describe how effectively the circuit is performing. Unlike other quantities, Power Factor is a ratio between 0 and 1. Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all.
teachChecks.hint: Remember that Z (Impedance) and X (Reactance) are measured in Ohms, but Power Factor is just a number with no unit.

      "hint": "Remember that Z (Impedance) and X (Reactance) are measured in Ohms, but Power Factor is just a number with no unit."

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: The SI System Framework

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the full name of the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering?
What term describes fundamental units, such as the Metre, that cannot be broken down into other units?
What term describes units that are created by mathematically combining fundamental building blocks?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is it critical for the electrical industry to use a single international system of measurement rather than local regional units?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures global standardisation
Prevents errors in design and safety
Measurements remain identical across different countries
Provides a universal language for engineering
teachChecks.hint: Think about which units are 'standalone' building blocks and which ones are built by combining others.

teachChecks.title: General Physical Units

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the standard SI unit for mass?
In scientific and electrical calculations, what is the standard SI unit for time?
What is the SI unit used to measure velocity?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the unit for Area (m²) classified as a derived unit rather than a base unit?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is mathematically combined from the metre
It uses the base unit of length twice
It is not a fundamental standalone building block
It represents a calculation of length multiplied by length
teachChecks.hint: Remember that area and volume are derived from the metre, while mass is always in kilograms for SI calculations.

teachChecks.title: Core Electrical Quantities

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the SI unit for electrical current?
What is the SI unit for electrical resistance?
What is the SI unit for electrical power?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the concept of Power differ from the concept of Energy in an electrical circuit?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Power is the rate of doing work
Energy is the total work done over time
Power is instantaneous while energy is cumulative
Power is measured in Watts while energy is measured in Joules
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the 'big four': Amps for flow, Volts for pressure, Ohms for opposition, and Watts for the rate of work.

teachChecks.title: Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the SI unit for frequency?
What is the SI unit for electrical energy?
What is the SI unit for resistivity?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must an electrician use resistivity (Ωm) instead of resistance (Ω) when comparing the conductive properties of different raw materials?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Resistivity is a property of the material itself
Resistance depends on the specific shape and length of a wire
Resistivity allows for calculations regardless of physical dimensions
It distinguishes the metal type from the finished component
teachChecks.hint: Frequency is about cycles per second, Energy is total work, and Resistivity is a material property measured in Ohm-metres.

teachChecks.title: AC Specific Quantities

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the SI unit for the total combined opposition in an AC circuit, known as Impedance?
What is the SI unit for inductance?
What is the SI unit for capacitance?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the Power Factor described as a dimensionless quantity with no unit of measurement?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is a ratio of two similar values
The units cancel each other out during calculation
It is expressed as a number between 0 and 1
It represents a relationship rather than a physical quantity
teachChecks.hint: Remember that Z (Impedance) and X (Reactance) are measured in Ohms, but Power Factor is just a number with no unit.

Grounded source text:
Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO2 - Standard units of measurement

Lesson: 202-2A - SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Topic focus: SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Prerequisites: 202-1A

Must-have topics: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO2.AC2.1: Identify and use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units
- 202.LO2.AC2.2: Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Lesson split rationale: Groups the identification of base SI units with specific electrical quantities to establish a standard measurement framework.

In-scope teaching anchors: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Out-of-scope topics: Measuring Electrical Quantities

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Identify appropriate instruments for measuring different electrical quantities **Range** - SI units for: length; area; volume; mass; density; time; temperature; velocity - Electrical quantities (SI): resistance; resistivity; power; frequency; current; voltage; energy; impedance; inductance/inductive reactance; capacitance/capacitive reactance; power factor - Electrical quantities (measurement): resistance; power; current; voltage; energy ---

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "The SI System Framework",
      "objective": "Distinguish between base and derived SI units.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "The Système International (SI) is the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering. It ensures that a measurement taken in one country is identical in value to one taken elsewhere, preventing errors in design and safety. Within this system, we distinguish between base units and derived units.",
        "Base units are the fundamental building blocks that cannot be broken down further. Examples include the Metre (m) for length or the Ampere (A) for current. Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about which units are 'standalone' building blocks and which ones are built by combining others."
    },
    {
      "title": "General Physical Units",
      "objective": "Identify SI units for length, mass, time, temperature, and velocity.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electricians must use standard SI units for physical measurements to ensure calculations for containment and cable runs are accurate. Length is measured in Metres (m). When we calculate the space inside a trunking or the surface of a wall, we use Area, measured in square metres (m²). For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).",
        "Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s)."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that area and volume are derived from the metre, while mass is always in kilograms for SI calculations."
    },
    {
      "title": "Core Electrical Quantities",
      "objective": "Identify units for current, voltage, resistance, and power.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "There are four primary electrical quantities used in every circuit calculation. Current is the flow of electrons, measured in Amperes (A), often shortened to 'Amps'. Voltage is the electrical pressure or potential difference that pushes the current, measured in Volts (V). Resistance is the opposition to that flow of current, measured in Ohms (Ω).",
        "Finally, Power is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into another form, such as heat or light, and is measured in Watts (W). It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. These four units form the foundation of Ohm’s Law and Power Law calculations used to size cables and protection devices."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the 'big four': Amps for flow, Volts for pressure, Ohms for opposition, and Watts for the rate of work."
    },
    {
      "title": "Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity",
      "objective": "Determine the units for frequency, energy, and resistivity.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Beyond the basic four quantities, we must understand how electricity behaves over time and within different materials. Frequency describes how many times an alternating current (AC) completes a full cycle every second; this is measured in Hertz (Hz). Energy represents the total amount of work done over a period of time and is measured in Joules (J).",
        "While resistance (Ohms) tells us the opposition of a specific component, Resistivity is a property of the material itself, such as copper or aluminium. Resistivity is measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm). This value allows us to calculate the resistance of any length or thickness of wire made from that specific material. Using the correct unit ensures we do not confuse the physical property of the metal with the final resistance of the finished cable."
      ],
      "hint": "Frequency is about cycles per second, Energy is total work, and Resistivity is a material property measured in Ohm-metres."
    },
    {
      "title": "AC Specific Quantities",
      "objective": "Identify units for impedance, reactance, and power factor.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, current is opposed by more than just standard resistance. We also encounter Reactance (X), which is caused by components like coils and capacitors; this is also measured in Ohms (Ω). The total combined opposition from both resistance and reactance is called Impedance (Z), which is also measured in Ohms (Ω).",
        "The components that cause reactance have their own specific units: Inductance is measured in Henrys (H) and Capacitance is measured in Farads (F). Finally, we use the Power Factor to describe how effectively the circuit is performing. Unlike other quantities, Power Factor is a ratio between 0 and 1. Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that Z (Impedance) and X (Reactance) are measured in Ohms, but Power Factor is just a number with no unit."
    }
  ]
}

{
  "title": "Worked Example",
  "objective": "Correctly identify and apply SI units to a set of circuit measurements.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a motor circuit and have recorded four different values. To complete the commissioning certificate, you must assign the correct SI unit or symbol to each value: the flow of electrons (15), the electrical pressure (230), the supply cycles per second (50), and the power efficiency ratio (0.8).",
    "Step 1: Identify the electrical quantity for each value. The flow of electrons is Current. The electrical pressure is Voltage. The cycles per second is Frequency. The power efficiency ratio is the Power Factor.",
    "Step 2: Match each quantity to its internationally recognised SI unit or symbol.",
    "1. Current is measured in Amperes (A).",
    "2. Voltage is measured in Volts (V).",
    "3. Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz).",
    "4. Power Factor is a ratio and is dimensionless (no unit).",
    "Step 3: Write out the final values with their correct designations.",
    "1. 15 A",
    "2. 230 V",
    "3. 50 Hz",
    "4. 0.8 (No unit)",

parsed.title: Worked Example
parsed.objective: Correctly identify and apply SI units to a set of circuit measurements.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting a motor circuit and have recorded four different values. To complete the commissioning certificate, you must assign the correct SI unit or symbol to each value: the flow of electrons (15), the electrical pressure (230), the supply cycles per second (50), and the power efficiency ratio (0.8).

Step 1: Identify the electrical quantity for each value. The flow of electrons is Current. The electrical pressure is Voltage. The cycles per second is Frequency. The power efficiency ratio is the Power Factor.
Step 2: Match each quantity to its internationally recognised SI unit or symbol.
1. Current is measured in Amperes (A).
2. Voltage is measured in Volts (V).
3. Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz).
4. Power Factor is a ratio and is dimensionless (no unit).
Step 3: Write out the final values with their correct designations.
4. 0.8 (No unit)
Final Result: The measurements are correctly recorded as 15 A, 230 V, 50 Hz, and 0.8.

Takeaway: Precision is key in electrical science. Using the wrong unit (like calling frequency 'cycles' instead of 'Hertz') or adding a unit to a dimensionless ratio like Power Factor can lead to confusion and errors in technical documentation.
parsed.hint: Focus on the 'big four' electrical units first, then remember that ratios like Power Factor never have a unit symbol.

Grounded source text:
Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO2 - Standard units of measurement

Lesson: 202-2A - SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Topic focus: SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Prerequisites: 202-1A

Must-have topics: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO2.AC2.1: Identify and use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units
- 202.LO2.AC2.2: Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Lesson split rationale: Groups the identification of base SI units with specific electrical quantities to establish a standard measurement framework.

In-scope teaching anchors: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Out-of-scope topics: Measuring Electrical Quantities

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Excerpt 1 (d463ec05-3286-49ee-a12b-b7336612ef31): ## LO2 — Standard units of measurement **AC** 1. Identify and use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units 2. Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities 3. Identify appropriate instruments for measuring different electrical quantities **Range** - SI units for: length; area; volume; mass; density; time; temperature; velocity - Electrical quantities (SI): resistance; resistivity; power; frequency; current; voltage; energy; impedance; inductance/inductive reactance; capacitance/capacitive reactance; power factor - Electrical quantities (measurement): resistance; power; current; voltage; energy ---

  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "SI Units",
      "simpleDefinition": "The international system of measurement used to ensure consistency across engineering and science.",
      "anchor": "The global language of measurement.",
      "misconception": "Thinking imperial units like inches or pounds are used in technical electrical calculations."
    },
    {
      "term": "Base Units",
      "simpleDefinition": "Fundamental units that are not built from other units, such as the Metre (length) and Ampere (current).",
      "anchor": "The fundamental building blocks of measurement.",
      "misconception": "Believing that all common units, like the Watt, are base units."
    },
    {
      "term": "Derived Units",
      "simpleDefinition": "Units created by mathematically combining base units, such as the Watt, Volt, or Joule.",
      "anchor": "Units built from base unit combinations.",
      "misconception": "Thinking derived units are less 'official' or accurate than base units."
    },
    {
      "term": "Resistivity",
      "simpleDefinition": "A property of a specific material that describes how strongly it opposes current flow, regardless of its shape.",
      "anchor": "Measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm).",
      "misconception": "Confusing resistivity (a material property) with resistance (the opposition of a specific object)."
    },
    {
      "term": "Power Factor",
      "simpleDefinition": "A ratio showing how effectively electrical power is being converted into useful work in an AC circuit.",
      "anchor": "A dimensionless number between 0 and 1.",
      "misconception": "Trying to assign a unit like 'Watts' to power factor when it is actually a unitless ratio."
    },
    {
      "term": "Impedance",
      "simpleDefinition": "The total opposition to current flow in an AC circuit, including both resistance and reactance.",
      "anchor": "Symbol Z, measured in Ohms (Ω).",
      "misconception": "Thinking resistance is the only thing that opposes current in an AC system."
    },
    {
      "term": "Energy",
      "simpleDefinition": "The total amount of work done or heat produced over a specific period of time.",
      "anchor": "Measured in Joules (J).",
      "misconception": "Using the terms 'Power' and 'Energy' as if they mean the same thing."
    },
    {
      "term": "Frequency",
      "simpleDefinition": "The number of complete cycles an alternating current (AC) makes in one second.",
      "anchor": "Measured in Hertz (Hz).",
      "misconception": "Confusing the frequency of the supply with the speed at which electrons travel."
    }
  ],

    "SI units provide a universal standard for the trade.",

    "Resistance is measured in Ohms, but resistivity is Ohm-metres.",

    "Interchanging Power (Watts) and Energy (Joules).",

    "Assuming all electrical units are base units.",

  ]
}

- guidedPractice retrieval and task must clearly mirror the worked example pattern.
- practice must be more independent than guidedPractice.
- Do not use these phases to smuggle in extra untaught chunk content that should have been its own teach/check section.

- answerGuidance arrays must be concrete and gradeable.
- Never return empty questionText, empty retrievalTextLines, or empty answerGuidance for guidedPractice or practice.
- If you are unsure, generate a narrower single-target task instead of leaving any field blank.
output.rawText:
{
  "guidedPractice": {
    "title": "Guided Practice",
    "objective": "Correctly identify and apply SI units to lighting circuit measurements by following a structured process.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "Scenario: You are checking a lighting circuit and have recorded four values: the opposition to current (2), the rate of doing work (100), the total work done over time (3600), and the efficiency ratio (1.0).",
      "Step 1: Identify the electrical quantity. Opposition is Resistance. Rate of work is Power. Total work is Energy. Efficiency ratio is Power Factor.",
      "Step 2: Match to SI units. Resistance is Ohms (Ω). Power is Watts (W). Energy is Joules (J). Power Factor is dimensionless.",
      "Step 3: Format the final values."
    ],
    "questionText": "Following the steps provided, write out the four values (2, 100, 3600, and 1.0) with their correct SI unit symbols or designations.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "2 Ω, 100 W, 3600 J, 1.0",
      "2 Ohms, 100 Watts, 3600 Joules, 1.0",
      "2Ω, 100W, 3600J, 1.0"
    ],
    "hint": "Remember that Resistance uses the Omega symbol (Ω) and Power Factor does not have a unit symbol at all."
  },
  "practice": {
    "title": "Practice",
    "objective": "Independently determine the correct SI unit symbols for specific technical electrical quantities.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "Scenario: You are performing a cable calculation for an industrial installation. You have identified the following technical values:",
      "1. Material resistivity: 0.0172",
      "2. Total AC opposition (Impedance): 0.5",
      "3. Circuit inductance: 0.01",
      "4. Supply frequency: 50"
    ],
    "questionText": "Provide the four values listed in the scenario with their correct internationally recognised SI unit symbols.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "0.0172 Ωm, 0.5 Ω, 0.01 H, 50 Hz",
      "0.0172 Ohm-metres, 0.5 Ohms, 0.01 Henrys, 50 Hertz"
    ],
    "hint": "Be careful with resistivity; it is not just Ohms. Also, remember the specific symbol for Impedance is the same as Resistance, and Inductance uses the 'H' symbol."
  }
}

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "The SI System Framework",
      "objective": "Distinguish between base and derived SI units.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "The Système International (SI) is the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering. It ensures that a measurement taken in one country is identical in value to one taken elsewhere, preventing errors in design and safety. Within this system, we distinguish between base units and derived units.",
        "Base units are the fundamental building blocks that cannot be broken down further. Examples include the Metre (m) for length or the Ampere (A) for current. Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about which units are 'standalone' building blocks and which ones are built by combining others."
    },
    {
      "title": "General Physical Units",
      "objective": "Identify SI units for length, mass, time, temperature, and velocity.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electricians must use standard SI units for physical measurements to ensure calculations for containment and cable runs are accurate. Length is measured in Metres (m). When we calculate the space inside a trunking or the surface of a wall, we use Area, measured in square metres (m²). For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).",
        "Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s)."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that area and volume are derived from the metre, while mass is always in kilograms for SI calculations."
    },
    {
      "title": "Core Electrical Quantities",
      "objective": "Identify units for current, voltage, resistance, and power.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "There are four primary electrical quantities used in every circuit calculation. Current is the flow of electrons, measured in Amperes (A), often shortened to 'Amps'. Voltage is the electrical pressure or potential difference that pushes the current, measured in Volts (V). Resistance is the opposition to that flow of current, measured in Ohms (Ω).",
        "Finally, Power is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into another form, such as heat or light, and is measured in Watts (W). It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. These four units form the foundation of Ohm’s Law and Power Law calculations used to size cables and protection devices."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the 'big four': Amps for flow, Volts for pressure, Ohms for opposition, and Watts for the rate of work."
    },
    {
      "title": "Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity",
      "objective": "Determine the units for frequency, energy, and resistivity.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Beyond the basic four quantities, we must understand how electricity behaves over time and within different materials. Frequency describes how many times an alternating current (AC) completes a full cycle every second; this is measured in Hertz (Hz). Energy represents the total amount of work done over a period of time and is measured in Joules (J).",
        "While resistance (Ohms) tells us the opposition of a specific component, Resistivity is a property of the material itself, such as copper or aluminium. Resistivity is measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm). This value allows us to calculate the resistance of any length or thickness of wire made from that specific material. Using the correct unit ensures we do not confuse the physical property of the metal with the final resistance of the finished cable."
      ],
      "hint": "Frequency is about cycles per second, Energy is total work, and Resistivity is a material property measured in Ohm-metres."
    },
    {
      "title": "AC Specific Quantities",
      "objective": "Identify units for impedance, reactance, and power factor.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, current is opposed by more than just standard resistance. We also encounter Reactance (X), which is caused by components like coils and capacitors; this is also measured in Ohms (Ω). The total combined opposition from both resistance and reactance is called Impedance (Z), which is also measured in Ohms (Ω).",
        "The components that cause reactance have their own specific units: Inductance is measured in Henrys (H) and Capacitance is measured in Farads (F). Finally, we use the Power Factor to describe how effectively the circuit is performing. Unlike other quantities, Power Factor is a ratio between 0 and 1. Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that Z (Impedance) and X (Reactance) are measured in Ohms, but Power Factor is just a number with no unit."
    }
  ]
}

integrative.title: Motor Specification Analysis
integrative.objective: Synthesize the understanding of material properties versus circuit performance by correctly identifying and applying SI units for resistivity and impedance.
retrievalTextLines: When specifying components like motors, we must distinguish between the physical properties of the materials used and the overall electrical behavior of the completed circuit. For example, the copper used in the windings has a specific resistivity, while the entire motor winding provides a total opposition to alternating current known as impedance.
Confusing these two quantities—or their units—can lead to incorrect cable sizing or motor selection.
integrative.questionText: A technical data sheet for a motor lists two values: a material property of 0.0172 and a total AC opposition of 14.5. Identify which value is the 'Resistivity' and which is the 'Impedance', providing the correct SI unit symbol for each. Briefly explain why these two quantities use different units despite both being related to electrical opposition.
answerGuidance: Resistivity is a property of the material itself (like copper), whereas Impedance is the total opposition of the specific circuit or component to AC flow, including resistance and reactance.
integrative.hint: Remember that resistivity describes the metal itself (Ohm-metres), while impedance (Z) describes the total 'fight' against AC current in the finished motor (Ohms).

steps.title: The SI System Framework
steps.objective: Distinguish between base and derived SI units.
keyIdeas: The definition of the Système International (SI) and the distinction between fundamental base units (like the Metre or Second) and derived units (like Velocity or Area).
Standardisation ensures that an electrical measurement in London means the same thing in Paris or New York, preventing catastrophic engineering errors.

The Système International (SI) is the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering. It ensures that a measurement taken in one country is identical in value to one taken elsewhere, preventing errors in design and safety. Within this system, we distinguish between base units and derived units.

Base units are the fundamental building blocks that cannot be broken down further. Examples include the Metre (m) for length or the Ampere (A) for current. Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: The Système International (SI) is the globally recognised system of measurement used in electrical engineering. It ensures that a measurement taken in one country is identical in value to one taken elsewhere, preventing errors in design and safety. Within this system, we distinguish between base units and derived units.

Base units are the fundamental building blocks that cannot be broken down further. Examples include the Metre (m) for length or the Ampere (A) for current. Derived units are created by mathematically combining these base units. For example, Velocity is a derived unit because it combines length and time (metres per second). In the electrical trade, most units we use daily, like the Watt or the Volt, are derived from a small set of these fundamental base units.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: All units are 'base' units. | Correct belief: Most units we use in the trade are derived from a small set of fundamental base units.
Think about which units are 'standalone' building blocks and which ones are built by combining others.

steps.title: General Physical Units
steps.objective: Identify SI units for length, mass, time, temperature, and velocity.
keyIdeas: Standard units for length (m), area (m²), volume (m³), mass (kg), density (kg/m³), time (s), temperature (K/°C), and velocity (m/s).
Electricians don't just work with wires; we calculate volumes for containment, weights for fixings, and distances for voltage drop.

Electricians must use standard SI units for physical measurements to ensure calculations for containment and cable runs are accurate. Length is measured in Metres (m). When we calculate the space inside a trunking or the surface of a wall, we use Area, measured in square metres (m²). For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).

Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s).
Explanation B

For the total space inside a room or tank, we use Volume, measured in cubic metres (m³).

Mass, which is the amount of matter in an object, is measured in Kilograms (kg). Density describes how much mass is in a specific volume, measured in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). Time is always measured in Seconds (s) for scientific calculations. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). Finally, Velocity describes speed in a specific direction, measured in metres per second (m/s).
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: Imperial units like inches or pounds are acceptable in technical SI calculations. | Correct belief: Only SI units should be used for formal electrical science calculations.
Remember that area and volume are derived from the metre, while mass is always in kilograms for SI calculations.

steps.title: Core Electrical Quantities
steps.objective: Identify units for current, voltage, resistance, and power.
keyIdeas: The Ampere (A), Volt (V), Ohm (Ω), and Watt (W).
These are the 'big four' quantities used daily for circuit design, fault finding, and component selection.

There are four primary electrical quantities used in every circuit calculation. Current is the flow of electrons, measured in Amperes (A), often shortened to 'Amps'. Voltage is the electrical pressure or potential difference that pushes the current, measured in Volts (V). Resistance is the opposition to that flow of current, measured in Ohms (Ω).

Finally, Power is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into another form, such as heat or light, and is measured in Watts (W). It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. These four units form the foundation of Ohm’s Law and Power Law calculations used to size cables and protection devices.
Teach the core idea: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units.
anchorFacts: There are four primary electrical quantities used in every circuit calculation. Current is the flow of electrons, measured in Amperes (A), often shortened to 'Amps'. Finally, Power is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into another form, such as heat or light, and is measured in Watts (W). It is vital to distinguish Power from Energy; Power tells us how fast work is being done at a specific moment. These four units form the foundation of Ohm’s Law and Power Law calculations used to size cables and protection devices.

steps.title: Frequency, Energy, and Resistivity
steps.objective: Determine the units for frequency, energy, and resistivity.
keyIdeas: The Hertz (Hz) for frequency, Joule (J) for energy, and Ohm-metre (Ωm) for resistivity.
Resistivity is a material property essential for calculating cable resistance, while frequency is critical for AC system stability.

Beyond the basic four quantities, we must understand how electricity behaves over time and within different materials. Frequency describes how many times an alternating current (AC) completes a full cycle every second; this is measured in Hertz (Hz). Energy represents the total amount of work done over a period of time and is measured in Joules (J).

While resistance (Ohms) tells us the opposition of a specific component, Resistivity is a property of the material itself, such as copper or aluminium. Resistivity is measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm). This value allows us to calculate the resistance of any length or thickness of wire made from that specific material. Using the correct unit ensures we do not confuse the physical property of the metal with the final resistance of the finished cable.
Teach the core idea: Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities.

steps.title: AC Specific Quantities
steps.objective: Identify units for impedance, reactance, and power factor.
keyIdeas: Impedance (Z) and Reactance (X) measured in Ohms (Ω); Inductance (H); Capacitance (F); and Power Factor (dimensionless/ratio).
In AC circuits, resistance isn't the only thing opposing current; we must account for the effects of coils and capacitors.

In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, current is opposed by more than just standard resistance. We also encounter Reactance (X), which is caused by components like coils and capacitors; this is also measured in Ohms (Ω). The total combined opposition from both resistance and reactance is called Impedance (Z), which is also measured in Ohms (Ω).

The components that cause reactance have their own specific units: Inductance is measured in Henrys (H) and Capacitance is measured in Farads (F). Finally, we use the Power Factor to describe how effectively the circuit is performing. Unlike other quantities, Power Factor is a ratio between 0 and 1. Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all.
Teach the core idea: 202.LO2.AC2.1.
anchorFacts: In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, current is opposed by more than just standard resistance. Finally, we use the Power Factor to describe how effectively the circuit is performing. Unlike other quantities, Power Factor is a ratio between 0 and 1. Because it is a ratio of two similar power values, it is 'dimensionless,' meaning it has no unit of measurement at all.

Remember that Z (Impedance) and X (Reactance) are measured in Ohms, but Power Factor is just a number with no unit.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Correctly identify and apply SI units to a set of circuit measurements.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a motor circuit and have recorded four different values. To complete the commissioning certificate, you must assign the correct SI unit or symbol to each value: the flow of electrons (15), the electrical pressure (230), the supply cycles per second (50), and the power efficiency ratio (0.8).

Step 1: Identify the electrical quantity for each value. The flow of electrons is Current. The electrical pressure is Voltage. The cycles per second is Frequency. The power efficiency ratio is the Power Factor.
Step 2: Match each quantity to its internationally recognised SI unit or symbol.
1. Current is measured in Amperes (A).
2. Voltage is measured in Volts (V).

taskSkeleton.takeaway: Precision is key in electrical science. Using the wrong unit (like calling frequency 'cycles' instead of 'Hertz') or adding a unit to a dimensionless ratio like Power Factor can lead to confusion and errors in technical documentation.

steps.hint: Focus on the 'big four' electrical units first, then remember that ratios like Power Factor never have a unit symbol.

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Correctly identify and apply SI units to lighting circuit measurements by following a structured process.
keyIdeas: Guide the learner through a supported task.

Scenario: You are checking a lighting circuit and have recorded four values: the opposition to current (2), the rate of doing work (100), the total work done over time (3600), and the efficiency ratio (1.0).
Step 1: Identify the electrical quantity. Opposition is Resistance. Rate of work is Power. Total work is Energy. Efficiency ratio is Power Factor.
Step 2: Match to SI units. Resistance is Ohms (Ω). Power is Watts (W). Energy is Joules (J). Power Factor is dimensionless.
Step 3: Format the final values.

steps.title: Motor Specification Analysis
steps.objective: Synthesize the understanding of material properties versus circuit performance by correctly identifying and applying SI units for resistivity and impedance.
keyIdeas: Integrative

When specifying components like motors, we must distinguish between the physical properties of the materials used and the overall electrical behavior of the completed circuit. For example, the copper used in the windings has a specific resistivity, while the entire motor winding provides a total opposition to alternating current known as impedance.
Confusing these two quantities—or their units—can lead to incorrect cable sizing or motor selection.
Using one short explanation, explain why Ωm is the correct technical answer for this 0.0172 is Resistivity, measured in Ohm-metres. scenario.

For example, the copper used in the windings has a specific resistivity, while the entire motor winding provides a total opposition to alternating current known as impedance.
Confusing these two quantities—or their units—can lead to incorrect cable sizing or motor selection.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review: Foundations of Current
spacedReview.objective: To recall the atomic basis of electricity and the movement of charge before applying standard units of measurement.
retrievalTextLines: Everything in the trade starts with the atom.
Protons are positive, neutrons are neutral, and electrons are negative.
In a conductor, 'free electrons' in the outer shell are easily knocked loose.
Electrical current is simply the directed flow of these free electrons through a circuit.
spacedReview.questionText: Before we start measuring quantities in Amps or Volts, what is the specific sub-atomic particle that must move to create an electric current, and what do we call a material that allows this to happen easily?
answerGuidance: The learner must identify the 'electron' (specifically the free electron) as the moving particle.
The learner must identify a 'conductor' as the material that allows this flow.

{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "SI Units",
      "simpleDefinition": "The international system of measurement used to ensure consistency across engineering and science.",
      "anchor": "The global language of measurement.",
      "misconception": "Thinking imperial units like inches or pounds are used in technical electrical calculations."
    },
    {
      "term": "Base Units",
      "simpleDefinition": "Fundamental units that are not built from other units, such as the Metre (length) and Ampere (current).",
      "anchor": "The fundamental building blocks of measurement.",
      "misconception": "Believing that all common units, like the Watt, are base units."
    },
    {
      "term": "Derived Units",
      "simpleDefinition": "Units created by mathematically combining base units, such as the Watt, Volt, or Joule.",
      "anchor": "Units built from base unit combinations.",
      "misconception": "Thinking derived units are less 'official' or accurate than base units."
    },
    {
      "term": "Resistivity",
      "simpleDefinition": "A property of a specific material that describes how strongly it opposes current flow, regardless of its shape.",
      "anchor": "Measured in Ohm-metres (Ωm).",
      "misconception": "Confusing resistivity (a material property) with resistance (the opposition of a specific object)."
    },
    {
      "term": "Power Factor",
      "simpleDefinition": "A ratio showing how effectively electrical power is being converted into useful work in an AC circuit.",
      "anchor": "A dimensionless number between 0 and 1.",
      "misconception": "Trying to assign a unit like 'Watts' to power factor when it is actually a unitless ratio."
    },
    {
      "term": "Impedance",
      "simpleDefinition": "The total opposition to current flow in an AC circuit, including both resistance and reactance.",
      "anchor": "Symbol Z, measured in Ohms (Ω).",
      "misconception": "Thinking resistance is the only thing that opposes current in an AC system."
    },
    {
      "term": "Energy",
      "simpleDefinition": "The total amount of work done or heat produced over a specific period of time.",
      "anchor": "Measured in Joules (J).",
      "misconception": "Using the terms 'Power' and 'Energy' as if they mean the same thing."
    },
    {
      "term": "Frequency",
      "simpleDefinition": "The number of complete cycles an alternating current (AC) makes in one second.",
      "anchor": "Measured in Hertz (Hz).",
      "misconception": "Confusing the frequency of the supply with the speed at which electrons travel."
    }
  ],

Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO2 - Standard units of measurement

Lesson: 202-2A - SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Topic focus: SI Units and Electrical Quantities

Prerequisites: 202-1A

Must-have topics: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO2.AC2.1: Identify and use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units
- 202.LO2.AC2.2: Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Lesson split rationale: Groups the identification of base SI units with specific electrical quantities to establish a standard measurement framework.

In-scope teaching anchors: use internationally recognised base and derived (SI) units; Identify/determine values of base and derived SI units for electrical quantities

Out-of-scope topics: Measuring Electrical Quantities

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

issues.excerpt: "questionText": "What term describes units that are created by mathematically combining fundamental building blocks?", "answerGuidance": ["Base Units"]
issues.problem: The answer guidance is factually incorrect and contradicts the question. Units created by combining others are 'Derived Units', not 'Base Units'.
issues.whyItMatters: The system will mark correct learner answers as wrong and reinforce incorrect terminology.
issues.suggestion: Change the answer guidance for the third question in Teach/Check 1 to 'Derived Units'.

issues.excerpt: Using one short explanation, explain why Ωm is the correct technical answer for this 0.0172 is Resistivity, measured in Ohm-metres. scenario.
issues.problem: The question text is mangled and contains the answer within the prompt itself.
issues.whyItMatters: It removes the cognitive challenge and makes the task trivial, while appearing unprofessional to the learner.
issues.suggestion: Clean the question text to: 'Explain why Ωm is the correct unit for the 0.0172 value in this motor specification scenario.'

issues.excerpt: "answerGuidance": ["0.0172 Ωm, 0.5 Ω, 0.01 H, 50 Hz", "0.0172 Ohm-metres, 0.5 Ohms, 0.01 Henrys, 50 Hertz"]
issues.problem: Bundled multi-part task. The learner is asked to provide four distinct values and units in a single string.
issues.whyItMatters: If a learner gets 3 out of 4 correct, the entire response will be marked as incorrect. It is impossible to provide granular feedback on which specific unit was wrong.
issues.suggestion: Split the practice task into four separate basicQuestions or use a table-style input if supported.

issues.excerpt: "requiredOutputs": ["2 Ω, 100 W, 3600 J, 1.0"]
issues.problem: The guided practice requires a very specific comma-separated string format that is not explicitly instructed to the learner.
issues.whyItMatters: Learners may know the units but fail the check due to formatting (e.g., using semicolons or new lines).
issues.suggestion: Add explicit formatting instructions: 'List your answers separated by commas in the order they appear in the scenario.'
